title: Add Prediction Mode — 'Where will the bottleneck appear?' before simulation
description: The single highest-impact practice feature based on learning science (testing effect g=0.74 + surprise-driven encoding). Before running a simulation, prompt: 'Click the component you think will fail first under 10x traffic.' Student clicks a node. Then simulation runs and reveals the actual bottleneck. The gap between prediction and reality triggers enhanced memory encoding. Implement as an overlay that intercepts the Play button: (1) Show prediction prompt with node selection, (2) Record student's choice, (3) Run simulation, (4) At failure point, show comparison: 'You predicted [X], actual bottleneck was [Y]. Here's why: [explanation from narrative-engine].' Can be toggled on/off (default: on for first simulation of each template).
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction prompt appears before first simulation of each template
Student can click any node to predict the bottleneck
After simulation reveals actual bottleneck, comparison overlay shows prediction vs reality
Explanation from narrative-engine describes WHY the actual bottleneck occurred
Correct predictions award bonus XP (integrate with progress-store)
Can be toggled off in settings for experienced users

title: Add 'Design from Memory' retrieval practice mode
description: After completing a template tutorial, offer: 'Close the tutorial. Rebuild this system from memory in 10 minutes.' Based on retrieval practice research (g=0.74 — even failed attempts improve learning). Implementation: (1) Store the reference architecture on tutorial completion, (2) Clear the canvas, (3) Show only the requirements (not the architecture), (4) Timer starts, (5) On submission/timeout, overlay reference vs student's design, (6) Score: components recalled (%), connections correct (%), novel improvements (+bonus). Integrates with SRS: concepts the student forgot get scheduled for earlier review.
acceptanceCriteria: Mode accessible after completing any template's learnSteps
Canvas clears to blank with only requirements panel visible
10-minute timer (configurable)
On submission: side-by-side overlay showing reference vs student design
Score: component recall %, connection accuracy %, time bonus
Missed components feed into SRS for accelerated review

title: Add 'You ARE the Load Balancer' manual tracing mode
description: Let the student BE a system component. Based on generation effect research (strongest learning from producing vs receiving). Mode: requests appear at the entry point. Student must click which server to route each request to (round-robin, least-connections, or their own strategy). System tracks: distribution evenness, latency impact, overload occurrences. After 20 requests, show results: 'Your routing caused 3 servers to overload. Round-robin would have distributed evenly.' Teaches load balancing intuitively through DOING, not watching. Implement 4 scenarios: You ARE the Load Balancer, You ARE the Cache (decide hit/miss), You ARE the Rate Limiter (allow/reject), You ARE Chaos Monkey (choose what to kill).
acceptanceCriteria: 4 manual-tracing scenarios implemented (LB, Cache, Rate Limiter, Chaos Monkey)
Each scenario presents sequential decisions with visual feedback
Results screen compares student's decisions to optimal strategy
Explanation of WHY optimal strategy is better
XP awarded based on decision quality

title: Add architecture comparison challenges — 'Which design handles more load?'
description: Based on contrasting cases research: students learn concepts better when they compare two implementations. Implementation: show two architectures side-by-side (e.g., fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read, SQL vs NoSQL, monolith vs microservices). Student predicts which performs better on a specific metric. Then simulation runs on BOTH and reveals the answer with explanation. 5 initial comparison challenges: (1) Fanout strategies for Twitter, (2) SQL vs NoSQL for e-commerce, (3) Monolith vs Microservices scaling, (4) Cache-aside vs Write-through consistency, (5) Active-Active vs Active-Passive failover.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 comparison challenges with split-screen architecture views
Student predicts winner before simulation reveals answer
Simulation runs on both architectures with identical traffic
Results show metric comparison (latency, throughput, cost, availability)
Explanation highlights the specific design choice that caused the difference

title: Add 'Find the SPOF' bug-finding challenges
description: Based on desirable difficulty research: harder practice produces better retention. Show a professional-looking architecture with a hidden single point of failure, data loss risk, or thundering herd vulnerability. Student must identify the bug by clicking the vulnerable component. 4 initial challenges: (1) Payment system with no idempotency on retry, (2) Chat system with no message persistence before WebSocket, (3) CDN with synchronized TTL expiry (thundering herd), (4) Notification system with no dedup on retry. Each challenge includes a 'Run Failure Scenario' button that simulates the exploit.
acceptanceCriteria: 4 bug-finding challenges with hidden architectural vulnerabilities
Student clicks the component they believe is vulnerable
Wrong guesses show 'Not quite — this component is actually protected because...'
Correct guess shows the vulnerability with simulation demonstrating the failure
Explanation teaches the PATTERN (e.g., 'always dedup retries in distributed systems')

title: Add interleaved practice mode — mixed-topic sessions
description: Based on interleaving research (50-125% improvement over blocked practice, d=0.67). Current challenges are organized by topic (all caching together, all databases together). Add a 'Mixed Practice' mode that draws questions from ALL previously-studied topics in random order. This forces the student to identify WHICH pattern applies (the discrimination skill interviewers test). Implementation: 10-question sessions drawing from: concept recall (SRS), prediction (simulation), comparison (which is better), and bug-finding (find SPOF). Each question from a different topic area.
acceptanceCriteria: Mixed Practice mode accessible from main menu and daily challenge
10-question sessions with questions drawn from ALL studied topics
Question types mixed: recall, prediction, comparison, bug-finding
Each question from a different topic area (no two consecutive from same topic)
Results show per-topic accuracy revealing weakness areas

title: Add scaffolding-removal progressive difficulty for templates
description: Based on Vygotsky's ZPD and cognitive load theory. For each template, implement 4 difficulty levels that progressively remove scaffolding: Level 1 (Warm-up): Full architecture shown, student answers concept questions. Level 2 (Easy): Architecture with 2-3 components removed, student drags them into correct positions. Level 3 (Medium): Only requirements given, student builds from scratch. Level 4 (Hard): Requirements + 'traffic just 10x'd' — modify under pressure. This creates the 'desirable difficulty' that Bjork's research shows produces the strongest learning.
acceptanceCriteria: 4 difficulty levels per template with progressive scaffolding removal
Level 1: concept questions on visible architecture
Level 2: drag missing components into correct slots
Level 3: build from scratch given only requirements
Level 4: modify running design under traffic/chaos pressure
Difficulty level tracked per-template in progress store
Integrates with existing difficulty-adaptation.ts Bayesian model

title: Add 'Why did you choose that?' elaborative interrogation prompts
description: Based on elaborative interrogation research (76% vs 69% in controlled studies). After a student places a component or makes a connection, occasionally prompt: 'WHY did you choose Redis here instead of Memcached?' or 'WHY did you put the cache before the database, not after?' Student types a short justification. AI evaluates the reasoning quality (not just the component choice). This trains the EXACT skill that distinguishes senior from junior candidates: articulating trade-offs under questioning. Prompts should fire 1 in every 3-4 actions (not every action — that's annoying).
acceptanceCriteria: Prompts appear after ~1 in 4 component placements during challenge mode
Questions are contextual to the specific component and its position in the architecture
Student types 1-3 sentence justification
AI evaluates reasoning quality with brief feedback
Can be toggled off for free-build mode (only active during challenges)
Frequency configurable in settings

title: Add 'Design First, Learn Second' generation-before-instruction mode
description: Based on the generation effect (Slamecka & Graf, 1978): producing answers before instruction creates stronger memory than receiving instruction first. Before showing ANY tutorial content on a system design topic, give the user a blank canvas and say: 'You have 10 minutes. Design a [rate limiter / chat system / payment system]. Use whatever you know.' After submission, THEN show the tutorial with the reference architecture. The student's own failed attempt primes their brain to absorb the correct design more deeply. This reverses the current flow (learn → practice) to (attempt → learn → practice).
acceptanceCriteria: When user opens a template for the first time, option to 'Try First' before seeing the tutorial
Blank canvas with only the problem statement
10-minute timer
After submission or timeout, tutorial reveals with reference architecture
Overlay shows what the student got right and what they missed
Optional (not forced) — user can skip to tutorial directly
